FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study dataset release 5.1; U.S. sample of 4289 adolescents at 21 NIH recruitment sites, ages 11-14 across 3 waves (2018-2022).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-lagged panel models (CLPMs) and hierarchical mixed-effects models (HMEMs) examining directional associations between psychopathology and gaming disorder across 3 time points.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in JAMA Network Open 2025; no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found anywhere in the paper. The sole screen-related variable is gaming disorder measured via the Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), plus minutes gaming per week (Table 2). VGAQ is a generic video-game variable with no explicit mobile/phone modality specified. Per binding clarification 2, VGAQ and generic gaming hours fail IC2 unless explicitly mobile/phone-based or paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable. No MPIQ, SMAQ, social media time, phone time, or other qualifying phone/SM variable appears anywhere (main tables, Figure 2, Figure 3, or methods). Step 2: not applicable — no composite examined; the variable is VGAQ/gaming hours, which is gaming-only. IC2 = NOT_MET.

notes: Paper examines psychopathology (CBCL: depression, ADHD, anxiety, social problems, conduct disorder) <-> gaming disorder (VGAQ) using CLPM. No phone or social media variable present. Excluded under FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data release 5.1, U.S. sample, 3 waves (ages 9-11, 12-13, 13-14); N up to 3419.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis: linear mixed models testing parental factors -> sleep outcomes, with path-analytic mediation models and moderation analyses.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in JAMA Network Open 2025; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Screen use (assessed as total daily screen time hours via Youth Screen Time Survey at wave 2) appears exclusively as a mediator in mediation analyses — e.g., parental monitoring -> screen use -> sleep quality (indirect beta = 0.004); family conflict -> screen use -> sleep chronotype (indirect beta = 0.01). Screen use is never the IV or DV of a primary analysis; all primary models regress sleep outcomes on parental factors (warmth, monitoring, family conflict, parental psychopathology). Per binding clarification 7, SM/screen as covariate/mediator/moderator-only fails IC2. No analysis pairs a phone/SM variable as IV or DV with an individual-trait outcome or predictor. Step 2: not applicable. IC2 = NOT_MET.

notes: Screen use is a mediator (wave 2) between parental factors (wave 1) and sleep outcomes (wave 3). It is never IV or DV in a primary analysis. Excluded under FT-EC2 (SM mediator-only).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study (Release 5.1), 21 U.S. sites, N=4,754, baseline ages 9-10, year 4 ages 12-15.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: independent linear mixed-effects regression models prospectively testing whether individual-difference predictors (impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, cognition) at ages 9-10 predict screen time and problematic screen use of video games, social media, and smartphones at ages 12-15.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in JAACAP Open 2025; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Multiple phone/SM-specific estimates found. Table 3 reports beta coefficients for predictors of social media hours (SM-specific DV): urgency beta=+.06 (p<.001), reward sensitivity beta=+.08 (p<.001), externalizing beta=+.04 (p=.008), general cognitive ability beta=-.08 (p<.001), learning/memory beta=-.06 (p<.001). Table 4 reports beta coefficients for SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) as DV: urgency beta=+.05 (p<.001), punishment sensitivity beta=+.04 (p<.001). Table 4 also reports beta coefficients for MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire) as DV: urgency beta=+.10 (p<.001), reward sensitivity beta=+.08 (p=.003), externalizing beta=+.05 (p=.040), punishment sensitivity beta=+.06 (p<.001). Predictors on the other side are individual-trait-level variables (impulsivity facets, motivation/reward sensitivity, psychopathology, cognitive ability). IC2 = MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative beta coefficients, SE, and p-values reported in Tables 3 and 4 for social media hours, SMAQ, and MPIQ as DVs. Inferential linear mixed-effects models with numeric results throughout.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses (linear mixed-effects regression) report numeric estimates (beta, SE, p) for all SM/phone variables as DVs predicted by individual-difference traits. MET jointly with IC3.

FT_EC7_reason: All three EC7 conditions hold: (a) longitudinal design — baseline ages 9-10 predicting year 4 outcomes; (b) SM/phone variables (social media hours, SMAQ, MPIQ) are DV in every analysis; (c) no analysis anywhere in the paper uses SM/phone as IV — individual-difference traits are always predictors and screen/SM/phone use outcomes are always DVs. No cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis found (all analyses are prospective with baseline predictors and year-4 outcomes). Cross-sectional carve-out does not apply. EC7 exclusion applies.
notes: Paper prospectively predicts social media ST, SMAQ, MPIQ, video game ST, and VGAQ from baseline traits. Qualifying SM/phone variables (social media hours, SMAQ, MPIQ) are always DV; individual-difference traits are always IV. Fully longitudinal, no SM-as-IV analysis. Excluded under FT-EC7.
